text: The video shows a small right scrotal hernia that appears clinically reducible but is not fully reducible during the operation. The back wall of the hernia sac is visibly composed of the colon, identified as a cecal slider hernia.
The surgeon attempts to reduce the colon from the hernia sac, but numerous fibrous attachments are visible, hindering the full reduction of the colon.

The surgeon begins creating a peritoneal flap, utilizing a third instrument for enhanced retraction. A Cartier is placed in the left lower quadrant opposite the hernia site, allowing the left hand to assist the right hand in performing the dissection, mimicking the technique of open surgery.

The surgeon begins creating a peritoneal flap, aiming to reduce the hernia sac. The transverse salus fascia is visible against the rectus muscle. The third instrument is used for flap retraction, while dissection occurs laterally between the peritoneum and testicular vessels, including the vas deferens.

This way you will always know where the testicular vessel is and not injure it in case the dissection becomes less hemostatic. But make every attempt to keep the dissection hemostatic so that you can see the tissue planes and see the critical structures to dissect away from the hernia sac. And last but not least is to always follow the Ten Commandments, even in difficult scleral hernias.

The surgeon develops the peritoneal flap in the space of Retzius using blunt dissection, cauterizing small vessels as needed. The dissection proceeds laterally, avoiding excessive retro-rectus fat against the rectus muscle, and maintaining focus on the peritoneum to prevent exposure of the transverse salus fascia against the transverse abdominis.

The surgeon uses the third arm to assist in dissecting along the peritoneum, aiming to stay within the ideal plane. Visible blood in the surgical field is noted as originating from a tap block performed prior to the procedure.

The surgeon identifies and separates multiple layers from the peritoneum, revealing the cecum without a posterior peritoneal sac. Blood vessels and fat are meticulously removed from the peritoneum as the surgeon navigates deeper into the tissue layers.

The surgeon identifies the cecum, a partly retroperitoneal structure, sliding into the indirect space without a peritoneal sac. The dissection is performed close to the cecum, guided by small blood vessels that need to be separated from the hernia contents.

The surgeon uses the third arm to assist in precise dissection of microfacial layers, carefully removing small blood vessels to separate the testicular vessels from the hernia sac and contents. This technique maintains the testicular vessels against the retroperitoneum, minimizing the risk of injury if further dissection into the scrotum or inguinal canal is required.

The surgeon moves medially to connect the space of Bargros dissection to the space of Retzius dissection. The VAS deferens is visible and positioned more posteriorly than expected, requiring careful navigation around the eight to nine o'clock position.

The surgeon uses a third instrument to assist in precise dissection at the tip of the hernia, minimizing bleeding. The surgeon employs a scissor to gently push and identify tissue planes, observing subtle movements that indicate which layers can be divided.

The surgeon navigates through a hernia dissection, avoiding the hernia sac and identifying a clear areolar plane amidst scar tissue. This plane allows separation between the hernia contents and the testicular vessels, which are encountered later than usual in this dissection.

The surgeon identifies a distinct fascial layer, ensuring the testicular vessel remains in its position and is not pulled down with the hernia sac. The dissection proceeds cautiously, with each structure being divided carefully to avoid inadvertently cutting the testicular vessels.

The surgeon continues to reduce the hernia, focusing on locating the tip of the hernia sac, which remains unseen. The medial dissection is reserved for last due to its complexity, with the external iliac vein becoming more prominent as the dissection progresses posteriorly.

The surgeon identifies and reduces a cord lipoma, consisting of fibro fatty connective tissue, alongside the hernia sac. The dissection is performed anterior to the illipubic tract, aiming to avoid the genital and femoral branches of the genital femoral nerve.

The surgeon uses a third instrument for retraction to expose the hernia sac, carefully pulling tissue to the far left or right to reveal thinner layers. The tip of the hernia sac is visible, and the surgeon cautiously divides tissue attached to it, ensuring no injury to underlying structures such as the vas deferens or testicular vessels.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the hernia sac, removing excess fat while maintaining hemostasis. Dense scar tissue is encountered, and the surgeon cautiously separates the scar and medial attachments of the hernia sac. The vas deferens is revealed, confirming it was not inadvertently damaged during the dissection.

The surgeon separates a clump of fat from the hernia sac, ensuring it is managed separately as if it were a cord lipoma. The hernia sac is now completely reduced, and the surgeon proceeds to parietalize the cord, aiming for good posterior coverage. The peritoneum is carefully lifted off the posterior structures, revealing pink bladder tissue and distinct color changes in the fat layers.

The surgeon dissects across the midline, approximately two centimeters deep to Cooper's ligament, ensuring the mesh will lay flat in the retropubic space. The peritoneum and bladder are carefully separated posteriorly to facilitate the mesh transition from the retropubic space to the lateral pelvic space. A structure is encountered during dissection.

The surgeon identifies the obturator fat plug at the posterior extent of the dissection, ensuring ample space around the indirect defect. The surgical field is cleaned further to allow the mesh to lay properly in all directions.

The surgeon identifies and points out nerves, likely including the femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve, amidst extra fatty connective tissue or lipoma. The surgeon proceeds to clean and move this tissue away to ensure the mesh can lay flat against the pelvic sidewall.

The surgeon identifies the lateral frontal cutaneous nerve, ensuring it remains protected by a fascial layer to prevent injury. The mesh is positioned so it does not directly contact the nerves, demonstrating the presence of an additional layer that could be dissected but is left intact for nerve protection. The mesh used is a sixteen by twelve right-sided anatomical progrip.

The surgeon positions the mesh by placing the seam approximately two centimeters behind the posterior edge of the internal ring, aligning it at the level of Cooper's ligament. The mesh is tapped down to conform to the curvature of the pelvis, ensuring proper left to right coverage. The mesh is unfolded to maintain its position when the bladder rises, providing excellent lateral and cephalad coverage.

The surgeon encounters a large mass of colon, appendix, and terminal ileum encased in a sac-like structure. Attempts are made to explore and potentially free the colon by cutting bands, but the complexity leads to the decision to leave it intact. A 3-0 V-lock suture is used, and a third instrument assists in elevating the heavy colon to prevent tearing the lower flap during the procedure.

This patient went home after surgery and was given two tablets of Norco. And I usually put them on round the clock ibuprofen and Tylenol every six hours for three days. Here I seem to be losing pneumopartineum. I think the insufflation tubing became dislodged, but that's not going to stop me from suturing. I usually run it back two or three times, pull the suture top, and then release it, relax it, and then cut it flush. This way there's no risk of the tail coming, flipping back out and causing a bowel obstruction. So thank you for watching.
